data.title: Canadian Inflation Comments
data.description:
So far, the gasoline price shock in Canada has not been as bad as what happened after the pandemic, but I am not incredibly optimistic about the medium-term outlook.

The above figure is the national average cost for unleaded gasoline (at self-service stations if you want to get even more specific), but the data ends in March, so it is missing the price rise since then.

In my neck of the woods in the Greater Montreal Area, the pump price has been around $2 per litre, although Montreal is normally higher than the national average series shown above.

The Carney government announced a temporary suspension of the excise tax on gasoline on April 14th, which dropped the price by about 11 cents per litre. This generated a lot of flak from economists, but I doubt that it will matter that much if the situation does not improve in the Middle East.

The Canadian CPI numbers for March were released today, and core inflation remains stuck at a level above the 2% target. This starting point makes it hard for the Bank of Canada to be too complacent in the face of commodity prices.

The diplomatic situation around the Strait of Hormuz seems muddled, but it is clear that full traffic flow will not resume immediately. This means that oil and gas fields will be shut in for an extended period, and restarting them will take time.

As such, it is clear that regions that are dependent upon the commodity flows from the Gulf are going to be hit hard. Canada is not directly affected, but it will see a lagged effect of slowing growth elsewhere.

At the same time, the signals from the White House remain belligerent towards Canada, and an attack on the Canada/Mexico/United States trade pact may occur once attention is drawn again to that topic.

The Bank of Canada’s next scheduled policy rate announcement is April 29th, and I see no reason for them to switch from their last assessment that growth risks are skewed to the downside, while inflation risks are to the upside. In the absence of clear data, it seems unlikely that they will move in the near run.

Fiscal Policy?

Although the gasoline excise tax cut was unpopular with economists, it is still a limited measure that targets the fastest-rising price in the economy. My uneducated guess is that fiscal policy is going to deviate much from already announced plans.

There are currently no measurable growth risks that will cause panic loosening of policy. A global recession due to the commodity shock would take time to hit Canada (and as a commodity exporter, some sectors of the economy will benefit from higher commodity prices). A complete rupture of free trade with the United States is a scenario that might provoke a more rapid fiscal reaction.

Without accommodative fiscal policy, the commodity price hike will tend to squeeze consumers, and so there might not be second-round price effects. (Although Canadian commodity exports would benefit, the employment in primary industries is not large enough to greatly push the overall labour market.)

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Silver And Gold Intraday Outlook: Metals In Focus With Ceasefire Uncertainty
data.description:
By Elior Manier

After rebounding sporadically over the past two weeks, gold and other precious commodities have failed to match the broadly positive, euphoric mood currently driving equity markets.

While they initially profited from the first corrective wave in the US dollar, capital flows have aggressively pivoted toward risk-on assets.

Traditional safe havens, which arguably lost their clear directional sight during the height of the war's panic, are now heavily questioning their role in this new environment - and investors are doing exactly the same.

The geopolitical clock is ticking. The temporary US-Iran ceasefire is officially set to expire by Wednesday, April 22, and both sides appear increasingly reluctant to extend the truce without a finalized, signed agreement in place.

The US administration is eager for a peaceful resolution, but the president communicated that he is also ready to use strength. Despite crude oil dropping back to the $90 handle, metals are struggling to pick up any meaningful bullish momentum while in this tense waiting room.

Should the ceasefire unexpectedly collapse without a diplomatic resolution, precious metals could face a violent, binary reaction. If WTI oil manages to remain contained below the critical $100 mark, gold could absolutely explode higher on a sudden rush of risk-off haven flows, capitalizing on its recent technical correction, and a lack of worsening inflation expectations.

Conversely, more risk-sensitive, industrial-leaning metals like silver and copper would likely struggle to catch a sustained bid, facing heavy downward pressure precisely because they are hovering near their current relative highs.

Let's explore the recent shifts in an intraday timeframe analysis of gold (XAU/USD) and silver (XAG/USD) to identify where the key levels are to watch for breakouts.

Gold (XAU/USD) 4H Chart and levels

Gold has rallied a quite impressive 17% after reaching 4-month lows on March 22 but has failed to breach the quintessential $4,900 resistance.

For bulls to retake the intermediate momentum advantage, they will have to generate a proper push above the psychological level – that could potentially happen if sentiment sours further.

In the immediate outlook, however, the 4H 200-period MA is putting bearish pressure; hence, if nothing fundamental changes, sellers would have the short-term advantage.

A break below the $4,781 50 MA confirms a turn lower and would push back towards $4,650.

Intraday timeframe levels to watch for gold (XAU/USD):

Resistance Levels:

  • $4,800 4H 200-period MA
  • $4,850 to $4,900 major resistance (bullish above)
  • $5,100 pivotal resistance
  • $5,400 mini resistance

Support Levels:

  • $4,781 50-MA short-term support
  • Daily momentum pivot $4,675 (bearish below)
  • Pivotal support: $4,325-$4,400
  • Main channel lows support: $4,100

Silver (XAG/USD) 4H Chart and levels

Silver has also rallied strongly from its March 22 lows but is now struggling to extend above the $83 resistance. Still evolving within a bull channel, traders will have to track its upper ($84.50) and lower bounds ($77) to play breakouts.

If the situation remains as confusing as it currently is, expect the channel to consolidate into a range between $77 and $83.

Higher timeframe levels to watch for silver (XAG/USD):

Resistance Levels:

  • Major resistance: $83 to $84.50 (mid-term bullish above)
  • Key range resistance: $90 to $92
  • $96.47 March highs (higher odds of all-time highs if break above)
  • Current record: $121.67

Support Levels:

  • Key momentum pivot: $75 to $79
  • 4H 50 and 200-period MAs ($77)
  • December FOMC minor support: $64 to $66
  • $61.10 past session lows
  • $50 to $55 October resistance now major support
  • Silver's 2011 all-time highs: $49.81

Safe trades and a successful week!

Original Post

data.title: Wise plc: Valuation Concerns Outweigh Benefits Of Upcoming NASDAQ Listing
data.description:
About this time last year, I wrote my first analysis of Wise plc (WIZEY), calling the stock a Buy. My opinion was based on the company’s potential for market share growth in its industry due to several macroeconomic factors, as well as my own experiences as a user of its services. Since that article was published on April 8, WIZEY has gained just over 20% in value, which is nothing to lose sleep about, except that the S&P 500 has appreciated about twice that amount during the same time period.

A new potential tailwind for the company is set to arrive next month as the UK-based financial services company expects to start trading on the NASDAQ marketplace on May 11. The listing will change almost nothing about the operations of the company itself, but it does open up the stock to potential higher trading volumes and more institutional investments from US firms. Wise also released its Q4 2026 Trading Update on April 13, giving me reason to revisit my initial research.

The company is showing impressive growth in active customers and account holdings. It seems to be continuously improving the speed of its currency transfers and decreasing costs for users. My biggest concern for the stock is its valuation, which seems to be slightly elevated in comparison to many of its peers. The market in which it operates is also incredibly competitive, which could cause it to drop the price of its services even more to keep adding users to its platform. As a Wise customer, I am still quite pleased with the value I receive for the services that I receive, but as an investor, I am somewhat less enthusiastic about buying more of its shares at their current price.

Company Overview

Founded in 2011 under the name TransferWise, Wise plc changed its name in 2021 to reflect its range of services that go just beyond the movement of currencies. The company now reports its operations in three separate divisions—Wise Personal, Wise Business, and Wise Platform. Wise Personal allows individuals to send and receive money in 160 countries and hold money in 40 different currencies. Wise also offers consumers the ability to use a multi-currency debit card that converts currencies at the mid-market rate.

Wise Business is for small and medium-sized businesses that need to send, receive, or hold money. It also offers accounting tools and payment options not available to individuals. Some funds held in Wise Business accounts may have the option of FDIC insurance through a pass-through program at partner banks. However, it is important to note that Wise plc itself is not a bank and does not offer any consumer or commercial lending services.

Wise Platform is a fintech solution for businesses that want to integrate Wise’s currency tools into their own applications. Wise plc has impressive partnerships in place with major financial players like Morgan Stanley (MS), Nu Holdings (NU), and Standard Chartered (SCBFY). In 2024, Morgan Stanley selected Wise plc to facilitate foreign exchange settlements for its corporate clients. In the same year, Nu Holdings, which operates under the name Nu Bank, announced that it would use Wise Platform to issue multi-currency accounts and debit cards.

The company’s biggest selling point is its ability to offer rapid currency transfers at low costs to its users. Almost 15 million customers and clients use Wise plc’s services every day. Almost three-fourths of the transfers it processes arrive at their account destination in under 20 seconds, and 95% arrive on the same day. The company highlights the fact that it uses mid-market exchange rates to lower the costs of its transfers. An example from its website on how a transfer of $1000 US dollars to euros would compare with Bank of America (BAC), Wells Fargo (WFC), and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) is shown below.

WIZEY vs. WPLCF

For the purpose of this analysis, I am writing about WIZEY. However, there are two ways for US investors to currently invest in Wise plc. The first is the US ADR, WIZEY. The second is the less liquid version of the stock, the ordinary shares that have the symbol WPLCF. Because both are currently traded on the OTC markets, there may be added costs for buying and selling shares in a US-based brokerage account. WPLCF has a lower average trading volume and may have wider bid-ask spreads.

At the time I am writing this analysis, WIZEY shares trade at about a 6% premium to WPLCF. Over the last three years, the ADR has outperformed the ordinary shares by about 10%. I would recommend limit orders for a transaction for either stock, at least until WIZEY is listed on a US exchange, but extra caution is advised for any investors trying to trade WPLCF. A market order for the ordinary shares could settle at a price notably higher or lower than the last transaction.

The Case for WIZEY

Because Wise plc is not yet traded in the US, it does not report its financial results in the same way that a domestic company would. The company releases detailed financial reports every six months. WIZEY released a Q4 FY2026 Trading Update on April 13 that contained the following information for the first three months of this calendar year:

  • Underlying income, or adjusted income, was approximately $588.2 million (£435.3 million), an increase of 24% year-over-year.
  • The company’s income is continuing to become more diversified as customer holdings grew by 37%, while card and other revenue rose 29% year-over-year.
  • The number of active Wise business customers grew by 26%, compared to the same period last year, to 572,000.
  • Q4 cross-border currency volume increased 26% to $59.5 billion (£44 billion) with 11.3 million active customers.

More detailed progress in these areas can be seen in the chart below.

Looking back at the last full report from the company, which it called its HY 2026 results, all of the company’s growth is not falling to its bottom line at the same rate. The company’s pre-tax margin fell 5.9% in the first half of FY 2026 and was lower than in the first half of FY 2024. Net profit before taxes had grown 51% between the first six months of FY 2024 and the same period of FY 2025. In the first half of FY 2026, it had fallen 13%.

The cause of this change is an increase in company expenses. General and administrative investments increased 35% from the first half of FY 2025 to the same period in FY 2026. Tech and development investments increased 18% during this time period. Marketing and sales costs ballooned 59% in the first half of FY 2026 compared to the same period from the previous year. In short, the company is paying more to run its business, invest for the future, and attract new customers. Many of these investments may very well pay off with greater sales and profits in the future, but there are no guarantees.

Peer Comparison

Wise plc operates in a very competitive market. For my peer comparison, I used the following five companies that are all involved in similar currency services. One from this group, Western Union (WU), still supports a significant physical network, but the company has become more involved in digital transactions in recent years.

  • Global Payments (GPN)
  • Payoneer Global (PAYO)
  • PayPal Holdings (PYPL)
  • Remitly Global (RELY)
  • Western Union

The first thing that I noticed is how differently these companies are treated by the market. I expected PayPal to attract the most attention, but each of these companies has a minimum of 10 analysts who cover their stocks. WIZEY has none.

Wise plc appears to currently trade at an elevated valuation based on a number of different financial metrics. Its PE ratio for the last 12 months of reported earnings is not too far out of range, but the difference would have been much greater if RELY’s valuation of 64.6 times earnings was removed from the equation. Western Union is currently valued at just 6.2. PayPal has a PE ratio of 9.39.

WIZEY

PEER AVERAGE

P/E GAAP (TTM)

29.63

25.65

Price/Sales (TTM)

6.57

2.57

EV/Sales (FWD)

5.25

2.31

EV/EBITDA (FWD)

16.95

8.49

Price to Book (TTM)

7.86

3.58

With other metrics like price to sales and enterprise value to sales, which were calculated on forward data, WIZEY is twice as expensive as its peer group. For price-to-book ratio, Wise’s closest peer is 4.85 from Remitly Global. Not all of these ratios hold equal weight in my book for what the stock from Wise is truly worth, but it is just hard to find any great justification for its current stock price when looking at other companies that conduct similar business operations.

Risks to Consider

Competition in the fintech payments space is fierce, with a number of players who have greater financial resources than Wise plc. The company will be under constant pressure to innovate and stay ahead of the competition. Innovation and advancements in technology create added expenses for a company that is already seeing pressure on its profit growth. Increased use of digital currencies could also impact the company, as it currently has a policy that does not allow the purchase or trading of cryptocurrency assets. Wise does not allow customers to send money to crypto wallets, receive money from peer-to-peer crypto trading, or hold money on behalf of individuals or businesses that trade virtual currencies.

An overall slowdown in the world economy, which could be caused by trade conflicts, logistical issues caused by the current situation in the Middle East, or any other number of macro headwinds, could impact the bottom line for WIZEY. Declining economic activity could lead to a decrease in the demand for the company’s currency transfer services. It could also cause current customers to pull money out of their Wise accounts.

The company is also adding a small bit of risk with its upcoming NASDAQ listing. There will be greater compliance standards for the company and added fees to meet new legal and accounting standards. Should WIZEY fall out of compliance with any of these standards, its listing could be suspended, damaging the company’s reputation.

Conclusion

The recent updates on Wise plc's operations and its listing in the US have had a clear impact on the stock. WIZEY has gained almost 23% in the last month. It has increased over 12% in value over just the last week. The stock could see additional short-term gains heading into May, similar to the impact that a stock in the US could see if it were to be added to one of the major indices. The fundamentals of the company are not changing, however, which is why I believe any new short-term pop in the price of the stock could be met with a reversion to the mean over the next several weeks after it is listed.

I am still a believer in the company as a customer, and I am still holding on to the relatively small amount of shares that I do own myself for now. The company is continuing to grow and innovate in a market that can change in a relatively short period of time. I just do not see enough supporting evidence to add to my position; hence, my rating change to a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ECB Waits For Signals From The Economy. War With Iran Raises Risks To Inflation And Growth In The Euro Area
data.description:
By Krzysztof Kamiński

The European Central Bank is still unable to clearly assess how strong the full impact of the war with Iran will be on the euro area economy. As Álvaro Santos Pereira, a member of the ECB’s Governing Council, emphasizes, the conflict is too recent to draw firm conclusions, so for now the central bank should closely monitor incoming data and refrain from hasty reactions.

Too early for a full assessment of the conflict’s effects

According to Pereira, the current situation requires caution because this is a classic supply shock. Such shocks usually lead at the same time to weaker economic growth and higher inflation, putting the central bank in a particularly difficult position.

On the one hand, energy and commodity prices are rising; on the other, economic activity is weakening. For the euro area, this means worsening conditions, although at this stage the negative impact of the conflict is not yet seen as dramatic.

A supply shock puts the ECB in a difficult position

Pereira notes that the euro area economy is currently somewhere between the ECB’s baseline scenario and the adverse scenario it had considered. Even before the current crisis, economic growth in the euro area had been running at around 1%, which in itself pointed to limited recovery momentum.

In this situation, any additional external shock, especially one related to the energy market and geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, increases the risk of a further weakening in economic conditions.

The ECB is paying particularly close attention to developments surrounding the fighting in the Middle East and the potential consequences for Europe of a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. This channel could be of key importance for oil prices, transport costs, and more broadly for cost pressures in the economy.

For the central bank, however, the most important issue will not be the temporary rise in prices itself, but the possibility of so-called second-round effects. This refers to a situation in which higher energy and commodity prices begin to spread into other sectors of the economy, pushing inflation higher in a more lasting way.

The biggest risk is entrenched inflationary pressure

In Pereira’s view, only clear signs that higher inflation is becoming entrenched, along with rising inflation expectations, should prompt the ECB to react. If such signals appear in the data, the central bank will have to respond. If, however, price pressure proves limited and temporary, it will be more appropriate to continue observing the situation and make decisions with great caution. This is particularly important at a time when less than two weeks remained before the ECB’s next interest-rate decision.

Europe needs not only caution, but also reforms

In the Governing Council member’s opinion, short-term caution in monetary policy should not obscure the broader picture. Pereira points out that for more lasting growth, Europe also needs structural measures, above all faster completion of the single market. Deeper economic integration could increase Europe’s resilience to external shocks and improve its long-term growth prospects.

For now, the ECB therefore remains in a mode of vigilant observation. The conflict with Iran is already worsening economic conditions in the euro area, but the scale of its impact has not yet been determined. The coming weeks and incoming data will show whether this is a temporary disruption or the start of stronger and more persistent inflationary pressure that would force the central bank to respond.

Original Post

data.title: Axon Enterprise: Impressive Growth, Real Margin Work Left
data.description:
Shares of Axon Enterprise (AXON) have lost half their value from a peak over the $800 mark in the summer. Despite such a pullback, shares have seen flattish returns since the early autumn of 2024, when I concluded that investors should not get tasered.

This pullback is seen in response to the company having posted record sales results recently, having demonstrated impressive growth in 2025. While this growth and strong sales guidance is comforting, lack of realistic profits due to excessive stock-based compensation expenses is a real issue, although such costs are seen coming down a bit in 2026, and hopefully in the coming years.

With shares cut in half, sales multiples remain demanding, as greater operating leverage is required to see appeal here, with strong topline momentum expected here. Appeal is far from being seen at this point, yet the pullback looks compelling, with more operating leverage required here.

Other interesting growth compounders are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

A Strong 2025

In February, Axon reported strong results for 2025, at least topline results. Reported sales rose by 33% to $2.78 billion. Product revenue rose by 29% to $1.58 billion, with service revenue up nearly 40% to $1.20 billion. That revenue composition helps in boosting margins, with the cost of services, of course, being much lower.

Despite gross margins being up 34%, the company suffered from higher operating expenses as GAAP operating profit of $58 million in 2024 turned into a loss of $62 million in 2025. The company reported GAAP earnings of $125 million, but the quality of this growth was poor, driven by a tax refund and large other income.

The company posted an adjusted profit of $564 million, or $6.85 per share, but I have real issues with that profitability metric. While I am happy to adjust for some smaller items, a $610 million pre-tax stock-based compensation expense is not among them, amidst minimal heavily adjusted earnings otherwise.

Amidst a flattish net debt load, an outstanding share base of about 83 million shares works down to a +$33 billion enterprise valuation. This is equivalent to about 12 times sales, although a near $800 million revenue number for the fourth quarter already trends at $3.2 billion, reducing the sales multiple to 10 times, amidst essentially break-even results reported.

Continued Growth

For 2026, the company calls for growth to continue at an impressive pace. Sales are seen up 27-30%, with adjusted EBITDA margins seen around 25.5% of sales. At the midpoint of the guidance, that suggests adjusted EBITDA of $911 million, which is ironic given the nature of the activities, compared to a $710 million number in 2025.

Comforting is that stock-based compensation expenses are seen as flattish around $605 million; this number is actually down minimally from a $610 million number in 2025, revealing that real profits are seen here. This is very encouraging, in the sense that real profits are seen here, these, however, being minimal in relation to a $33 billion enterprise valuation, even after shares cut in half from last year's highs.

Based on the guidance, sales are expected to surpass $3.5 billion this year, reducing the sales multiple to about 9 times here, and non-meaningful earnings multiples far exceed a hundred times here.

Safety Technology, Not Necessarily A Safe Investment

Axon's mission is to transform public safety by the usage of technology. The vision includes a world without bullets, a reduction in social conflict, and people having access to a fair and effective justice system.

The company aims to do this with its well-known Taser system, a less-lethal option compared to guns, and other services, including body cameras, in-car cameras, drones, and related software. Most of this is driven by the US, where the company generates 85% of sales in 2024, a number gradually seen coming down to 81% in the final quarter of 2025.

Founder-led, Axon has seen strong growth in the past. Over the past decade, revenues have increased tenfold here, with the company managing growth with margins that are essentially seen near break-even points, although the company has incurred about 50% dilution over this period of time.

Even down 50% from the highs, valuation multiples have expanded a great deal, having increased by more than 20 times over the past decade, indicating real valuation multiple expansion despite shares cut in half. Shares have fallen to levels first seen in the fall of 2024. Shares have been falling relentlessly recently but are up meaningfully in the long haul, as investors are catching up with reality, and they are not necessarily buying into the AI strategy employed by the business.

What Now?

Compared to the situation in September 2024, when I was cautious amidst nonexistent realistic earnings, something has changed here. At the time, the company traded at 14 times annualized sales; this multiple has come down to 10 times here, or 9 times if we look at the guidance for the year. Amidst no realistic profits seen here, expectations remain high, but multiples have come down quite a bit since 2024.

So here we are, with still no realistic earnings to be reported, but this is about to change for the better based on the 2026 guidance, with stock-based compensation seen as flat amidst impressive topline sales growth. That is comforting, but those earnings are still too minimal to provide support to the investment case. On the other hand, it is comforting to see the midpoint of stock-based compensation expenses being doled out amidst continued sales growth and dominance in the field.

This makes me more upbeat compared to the situation at equivalent levels nearly two years ago, yet that is just a minimal improvement in relation to a valuation that is still highly demanding. Valuations are still too demanding for me to buy a 50% dip from a too-far momentum-induced run higher before. However, there is more clarity to the future, with Axon reporting a strong 46% increase in 2025 bookings to $7.4 billion, adding rapidly to the backlog.

Besides, the company has provided strong 2028 revenue guidance, which calls for revenues around $6 billion. With sales seen near $3.6 billion in 2026, that suggests average revenue growth around 30% in both 2027 and 2028, in line with the guidance given this year and growth reported last year. This makes me upbeat, yet EBITDA margins are only seen up about 250 basis points in 2028, which only tells us part of the story as the question of how stock-based compensation will evolve. On that front, the company sees about 2.5% dilution each year in relation to the value of the firm, but that is not really telling that much.

After all, a current $33 billion enterprise valuation implies $800 million in stock-based compensation expenses, up from around $600 million actually guided this year. All in all, impressive topline growth is seen, yet operating leverage appears to be modest in the year to come, making me hard-pressed to see immediate appeal.

data.title: Digital Realty: Winning By Losing AI Buildout
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The way the AI infrastructure buildout has unraveled has shaped the sector's risk/reward distribution. When demand surged, it was the wildcatters of the cryptocurrency market that contributed heavily to the buildout - the bosses of the digital currency mining companies, who I dare say have a disposition for risk-taking. CoreWeave (CRWV), the largest pure-play generative AI hyperscaler, traces its roots to crypto mining. Hut 8 (HUT), HIVE Digital (HIVE), Core Scientific (CORZ), and many others have shifted their business models from crypto mining to high-performance computing. Nebius's CEO noted that while hyperscalers such as Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (GOOGL) might have more GPUs, their count is in the hundreds of thousands, putting them in the same category as Nebius.

Beyond the noise of these small risk-takers leading the AI buildout lies Digital Realty (DLR), an established data center REIT with a long track record. DLR has demonstrated underwriting discipline, opting not to chase growth at any margin.

Although demand for computing capacity exceeds supply, what should be a seller's market hasn't translated to favorable unit economics for suppliers. That's because of the unique structure of the market, which I've discussed in previous articles (here and here) and will touch on in the coming paragraphs. But the point here is that raw compute has very slim margins. Neoclouds like Nebius and CoreWeave aren't unprofitable because of R&D investing or construction ramp. R&D is too small, while construction costs are capitalized in the balance sheet. The deep unprofitability of the new infrastructure providers suggests that they might have underwritten unprofitable raw compute contracts (or underestimated costs). CoreWeave emphasizes adjusted EBITDA as its preferred performance metric, which excludes key recurring expenses such as GPU depreciation and interest on debt funding, highlighting its aggressive accounting. Their access to GPUs hasn't translated to high margins because of the unique market structure, characterized by a powerful GPU supplier and established GPU end-users, with neoclouds and smaller infrastructure suppliers squeezed in the middle.

My pitch is simple. Even if the margins of these hyperscalers improve, DLR remains a favorable play because of its discipline. Many of the new HPC providers built their data centers in remote areas where power is cheap. This is marketed as a good thing, particularly after the power supply shortage and the grid stress. Having a data center in a remote area is okay for crypto mining and training a foundational generative AI model, but it's less than ideal when low latency is important. While some of these HPCs are claiming that they're seeing more inference loads, there will come a day when the inference latency becomes unacceptable, and being closer to the urban centers becomes more critical.

For now, people aren't complaining that ChatGPT and Perplexity take 40 seconds to give an answer. They will eventually, and remote data centers won't cut it. This is where DLR's advantage lies. It might not have the triple-digit growth of new market entrants and neoclouds, but that's because of their disciplined approach to the market, focusing on quality locations and reliable, utility-grade power.

Why DLR Deserves a Valuation Premium Over Neoclouds

DLR is losing market share to the new infrastructure providers: Neoclouds and new HPC infrastructure operators. In FY'25, CoreWeave reported 168% revenue growth, while Nebius delivered 479% growth. One might argue that the latter is a full-stack public cloud provider. I would say that's their inspiration, but not reality. At this point, nearly all of Neocloud's revenue comes from raw compute. At this point, the difference between DLR and CRWV/Nebius and others is that one owns the GPUs, and one doesn't.

What's driving the growth of neoclouds is access to power supply and, secondly, GPUs, not the public cloud software services they aspire to sell one day. Here's the point.

Some might point to the "success" of neoclouds in securing energy. Power transmission lines take time to be installed. There is also a long wait for utility-grade gas turbines. Some popular power architecture implemented by Neoclouds includes installing smaller Caterpillar (CAT) diesel and gas combustion engines in parallel, creating a large-capacity cluster. Another emerging solution is the hydrogen fuel cells, particularly the ones sold by Bloom Energy (BE), called the Bloom Box.

These solutions solve the immediate supply/demand imbalance, but they can be more expensive than the utility-grade gas turbines that are currently in short supply. DLR seems to be growing at the same pace as its utility partners, a steady and reliable growth.

Access to GPUs isn't an issue either. I believe that DLR can buy GPUs if it wants to, and I would bet that it can put its hands on the latest GPUs before hyperscalers if it commits to changing its business model from a pure data center REIT. Companies like CoreWeave and Nebius have access to the cutting-edge GPUs because of the frenemies situation between NVIDIA and its hyperscaler customers, most of whom are running their own microchip programs that directly compete with NVIDIA. A significant portion of Amazon's Bedrock cloud services is running on Trainium and Inferentia chips. Anthropic is buying 1 million of Alphabet's TPUs. This is creating tension between NVIDIA and its customers, a frenemies situation that means that NVIDIA is happy to allocate its highly sought-after GPUs to neoclouds and, perhaps, new market entrants like DLR in the unlikely event that it chooses to change its business model from a pure REIT structure. For now, the practical takeaway from these dynamics is my skepticism of any valuation premium over DLR because of the GPU or power access argument.

What's Driving DLR's Growth

DLR's growth is slower but more reliable, fueled by well-established secular trends, including a shift from on-premise server rooms to third-party hosting. Hybrid cloud architectures are on the rise to save costs on public cloud services that charge by the token and instance. For some enterprises, running the core computing load on their own servers while also being connected to the public cloud for certain services saves a lot of money. Some customers are required by law to host their customers' data on their own servers, so they just rent out space from DLR. The key point here is that DLR's customer base is diverse, and each has its own reason to buy rack space. This is contrary to the likes of CoreWeave and Nebius, who derive the majority of their revenue from a handful of hyperscalers. For DLR, only one customer represents more than 10% of revenue - most likely a hyperscaler representing 11.7% of revenue. Beyond that, it is mostly enterprises that entrust DLR to take care of their servers.

Profitability and Dividend Coverage

DLR adopts a capital recycling strategy. The company buys and develops properties, stabilizes them so that they're income-generating, before selling them at a profit. The revenue from this approach is lumpy. However, it does translate to valuable capital gains that management distributes, or, as tax and minimum distribution requirements allow, does reinvest some of the proceeds in growing the business.

The company's adjusted FFO excludes these gains, providing a clear picture of the rent income and reliable run rate. Key items excluded in FFO are depreciation, because, typically, real estate assets increase in value, and depreciation expense increases the gap between real value and GAAP book value. The $2.6 billion in adjusted FFO in 2025 is a decent step up from the $2.2 billion reported in the prior year. This annual run rate is also well above the dividends, which stood at $1.7 billion in 2025, after the dividend increase earlier last year.

Final Thoughts and Risks

DLR has adopted a more disciplined growth strategy, making it one of the most reliable data center infrastructure buildout plays on the market today. Equally important, there are signs that the company's growth is accelerating, fueled by digitalization trends, as enterprises adjust to the new realities created by generative AI. Next year, the company expects AFFO to grow by 8.8%, multiple times its 5-year historical average of 2.4%. This high growth, combined with the broader industry trends, suggests that DLR might be at an inflection point. Booking from <1MW customers rose by 35% at the end of 2025, further strengthening this conclusion.

The main downside risk to this bullish rating is DLR's high valuation. The ticker is up 31% this year, exceeding the growth in its profitability. These dynamics are demonstrated by Seeking Alpha's Quant Score of "F" on valuation. The table below compares DLR's valuation multiples with the Real Estate sector's median, showing a significant premium.

However, one might argue that this premium is justified by growth. DLR has a Quant Score of "A-" on growth, a solid performance compared to peers.

This bullish rating is conditional on the company sustaining its growth momentum and profitability levels. A rating downgrade is possible if dividend coverage ratios deteriorate, threatening the sustainability of distribution.

data.title: TE Connectivity: Technological Uncertainty Limits The Near-Term AI Upside
data.description:
Introduction

TE Connectivity (TEL) has experienced a dramatic re-rating in its share price, as the stock has received wide recognition from the market as an AI beneficiary. Previously, TE has been widely regarded as a mature business, and its share price has been relatively stagnant throughout the past few years; however, since April last year, the stock has practically doubled on the back of this new AI narrative. In my view forward, the stock is a Hold at its current valuation of 22x forward FY2026 P/E based on several key reasons:

First, the company is benefitting from the recent boom in AI, which has allowed the company to escape its long period of stagnation; TE supplies critical connectors used in AI servers, and its AI revenue is expected to double from $1.4 billion in 2025 to more than $3 billion by 2027. TE also has a relatively strong financial position, with an FCF conversion ratio of more than 100% and a solid balance sheet. However, the upcoming transition from copper cables to optical in the CPO architecture raises potential technical uncertainty in TE's growth story. Furthermore, the company is facing near-term industry headwinds in the Automotive segment, which will limit the growth in its Transportation Solutions.

Business Overview

TE Connectivity is one of the world's leading providers of connectivity and sensing solutions. The company was formerly known as Tyco Electronics; however, the company officially changed its name in 2011. TE Connectivity's products are used in a variety of end applications, such as automotive, aerospace, defense, medical, and energy-related applications.

Historically, the connectors market has been a relatively fragmented industry, with numerous competitors catering to different market niches. Besides TE, companies such as Amphenol (APH), Molex, Foxconn Interconnect (HKG:6088), Luxshare (SHE:002475), and Aptiv (APTV) directly compete with TE. The company competes, however, with its large scale relative to competitors and also through its High-Voltage Connector portfolio (which means the company has a strong market-leading position). Based on management's assessment, the size of the connector market is roughly $130 billion; with TE's $17.3 billion in sales for FY2025, that would give the company a roughly 13% market share of the industry, which is larger than many of its peers.

Product Mix and Revenue Split

TE's revenue base can be divided into two main applications: the first is its Transportation Solutions segment, which accounts for 54% of sales in FY2025. The main products for this segment are connectors used in traditional ICEs, EVs, and commercial transportation. Besides auto OEMs like General Motors or Volkswagen, the company also supplies connectors to Chinese automakers such as BYD, NIO, and XPeng. In the near term, Transportation Solutions faces headwinds from the broader auto industry stagnation, which is why the segment has been relatively stagnant in FY2025.

The other main segment for TE is its Industrial Solutions segment, which accounts for 46% of sales in FY2025. Unlike Transportation Solutions, which are relatively straightforward, the Industrial Solutions segment is a different mesh of products of various applications. The main applications are Digital Data Networks (DDN), Automated & Connected Living (ACL), Energy, Aerospace, Defense, and Marine (AD&M), and Medical. While the Transportation Solutions segment has been stagnant (-1% YoY), the Industrial Solutions segment grew $1.5 billion in FY2025 (+24% YoY) and is the clear driver of growth going forward.

TE Connectivity Long-Term Growth Target

FY2025 was the most exciting period for TE over the past few years. Prior to this, TE's revenue had been relatively stagnant at roughly $14-16 billion after its post-pandemic recovery was over. At its 2025 Investor Day, management has been signalling a decisive inflection point in its growth trajectory, which will end its period of stagnation. For the next few years, management has indicated that they are aiming to grow revenue at a 6-8% CAGR by organic means. On its bottom line, management is aiming for incremental operating margin expansion of 50-60 basis points each year, which will translate to double-digit EPS growth. This will be accompanied by a 100+% FCF conversion, with an expected one-third reserved for dividend payments and the other two-thirds reserved for M&A and share repurchases.

Figure 1: TE Connectivity's Long-Term Growth Target, Source: TE's 2025 Investor Day Presentation

Clearly, management is optimistic about its future growth prospects; however, you may be wondering whether such ambitious growth is achievable since the company has largely stagnated over the past few years. Backing this target are several structural growth trends that are allowing the company to decouple from its past periods of cyclicality. In the Industrial Solutions business, the main driver will be the DDN and Energy segments; for these sub-segments, TE is benefitting through the rising speed for interconnect of GPU clusters and the high-power requirements needed to run them. Meanwhile, for the Transportation Solutions segment, the main driver will be the rising Content Per Vehicle (CPV), which will allow TE to outgrow the broader auto market by 4-6% annually regardless of total industry unit sales. Combined, these factors will allow TE to meet its 6-8% growth target in the long term.

Scaling AI Infrastructure: Targeting >$3B in DDN Revenue by FY2027

Even though both the Transportation and Industrial Solutions segments are set for a multi-year structural expansion, the Industrial Solutions segment is the main catalyst for the company, as management is expecting this segment to contribute to 60% of TE's long-term growth target. The underlying reason for this trajectory is the recent boom in AI, which has been the main factor for the surge in DDN revenue (+70% YoY in FY2025). In the past, AI/Cloud-related revenue used to be a small part of revenue for TE; in 2023, AI/Cloud-related revenue only accounted for $300 million of TE's total revenue. Today, in 2025, that figure has reached $1.4 billion and is only expected to balloon further. Management is projecting over $3 billion in AI/Cloud-related revenue by FY2027, which will further increase its sales contribution.

Figure 2: TE Connectivity's Connector Opportunity in AI Servers, Source: TE's 2025 Investor Day Presentation

You may be wondering what TE's role is in AI datacentres? In AI servers, connectors are required to transmit three key areas: data, power, and signals. Here TE plays a key role, as they provide hyperscalers with the connectors needed to perform these functions. Combined, TE has a total of $870 in dollar content for each chip, which is divided across a few key product categories: backplane, interconnect, data cables, and power solutions.

Backplane (Up to $400 per chip): These are high-density connectors that link the different circuit boards within a server. They are used to form the connection between GPUs within the same rack (scale-up networking). High-speed Data Cables (Up to $250 per chip): These are specialized cables that are required to connect server racks with networking switches. Power Solutions (Up to $150 per chip): These are heavy-duty busbars and power connectors that are required to deliver massive electrical loads without overheating. Over-the-Board and Interconnect: These provide the internal pathways that connect chips directly to the board or other internal modules.

If we extrapolate this to Nvidia's GB200/300 architecture, that would imply an estimated total content of $62,640 per server rack ($870 x 72 GPUs). Although this may seem small compared to the millions Nvidia makes per server, looking at the total GB200/300 shipments in 2025 of about 25,000 racks, that would still imply a $1.6 billion TAM ($62k x 25k racks) from Nvidia servers alone. This $1.6 billion TAM does not include the TAM from ASIC servers, which is not immaterial either.

Content Growth Offsets Negative Auto Outlook

Looking beyond AI, management is also expecting a rebound in its Transportation Solutions segment to be a key catalyst. Although management is expecting total auto shipment volumes to decline to 88 million in 2026 (down single-digit), TE is still expecting to benefit off the long-term structural growth of its Auto segment. Namely, there are three main trends that will aid TE in achieving this goal: Data Connectivity, Electrification, and E-Mobility. The key focus will be the adoption of ADAS and LiDAR systems in automobiles - management has indicated that the penetration of L2-4 ADAS adoption is expected to increase from 30% in 2025 to 70% in 2030, which will require incrementally more high-speed data connectors. The rising penetration of EVs and Hybrids (xEV) is also expected to grow from 30% to 60% penetration by 2030; since an xEV has roughly 2x to 3x the connector content of an ICE vehicle, TE is expected to benefit from this trend.

All in all, this structural story is positive for the company; however, the guidance for mid-single-digit long-term growth is nothing to feel excited about in my opinion. With the auto industry still on the decline, it is still uncertain when we will be seeing an inflection point; the recent US-Iran war and macroeconomic uncertainty have only exacerbated this issue. On the other hand, other drivers, such as Energy, Commercial Transportation, etc., have relatively small revenue contributions for the group. Hence, the bigger driver for TE will still be its AI story; however, the AI story for TE is not all roses and rainbows, which I will explain in the next section.

Figure 3: TE Connectivity's Transportation Solutions Drivers, Source: TE's 2025 Investor Day Presentation

CPO Adoption: Potential Displacement of Traditional Copper Architectures

While copper-based connectivity has been TE's main growth engine, there is a significant long-term concern for TE, which is the impending adoption of Co-Packaged Optics (CPO). As data transmission is migrating from 1.6T to 3.2T, traditional copper backplanes are struggling to keep up due to signal loss and heat generation as the speed increases. In this case, optical engines have to be moved directly from the front panel to the chip package, which will potentially displace copper backplane connectors, which is TE's main AI server product. While the timeline for CPO adoption is still uncertain, given its supply chain issues, the market is expecting CPO adoption to happen in the next few years, which is why share prices of Lumentum and Coherent have skyrocketed these past few months. SemiAnalysis has some insightful reads on CPO if you are interested in the current debate.

However, this does not mean that this is the end of TE's story, as they have acquired a new CPO-related subsidiary - RAM Photonics. RAM Photonics is a company working on fibre optic interconnect, which is trying to solve the two main problems with CPO - precise fibre alignment and high-volume manufacturing. Together, TE and RAM photonics have developed new CPO-related products - its Co-packaged Copper (CPC) product and CPO-to-backplane solution. CPC is the middle ground between CPO and copper - CPC has lower power requirements compared to CPO, while it does not require the use of retimers like in copper to mitigate signal loss. TE is hoping that this hybrid solution will allow it to stay relevant in the ever-evolving AI space; however, there is uncertainty as to whether TE's solutions will be adopted. Ultimately the form factor decision is up to Nvidia and TE's Hyperscaler customers, which leaves TE at their mercy.

Figure 4: TE Connectivity's CPO/CPC Product Offering, Source: TE Connectivity News

Financials

When looking at TE's financials, we can see that the inflection point for TE occurred in 2025 when revenue crossed the $17 billion mark for the first time. While the Transportation Solutions segment has been relatively tepid throughout the 2021-2025 period, the Industrial Solutions segment has surged past its previous high during 2025, driven by the 73% YoY growth in DDN revenue. To highlight, the Communications segment in the past is now lumped under Industrial Solutions; for a fair comparison, the Industrial Solutions revenue from 2021-2023 also includes revenue from the Communications segment. From the figure, we can see that TE is far from being an AI pure play; the contribution of DDN and total AI revenue is still currently at the teens level. Although the company is benefitting from the AI story, its corporate growth rate lags behind its AI component peers.

For FY2026, management is guiding for growth to exceed their long-term organic growth target. For the full year, TE expects double-digit sales and EPS growth, which is backed by the record order book of $5.1 billion (book-to-bill of 1.1). Part of this growth will be inorganic, stemming from the company's acquisition of Richards. But the main driver is once again DDN and AI, and management has indicated that its AI order expectations are $200 million higher than their initial projection. Meanwhile, the Transportation segment, as highlighted earlier, is expected to suffer from broader industry headwinds. Nevertheless, I am expecting overall revenue to be flattish due to the CPV upgrades that will offset this decline.

Figure 5: TE Connectivity's Key Revenue Trend Metrics by Segment, Source: TE Connectivity 10-K

TE's primary appeal is its FCF conversion, which has been consistently above 100%. Although the company's top line has been stagnant, FCF has steadily improved from under $2 billion in 2021 to more than $3 billion in 2025. Noticeably, TE has spent $2.6 billion in acquisitions in 2025, which was mainly due to its Richards acquisition for a $2.3 billion all-cash deal, which will contribute to $400 million in revenue and about $140 million in EBITDA. This deal has resulted in a material increase in TE's net debt position of $1.5 billion. But arguably, this will not severely affect TE's leverage, as the company's net debt of $4.4 billion can easily be repaid with just 18 months of FCF. Furthermore, TE's net debt-to-EBITDA ratio stands at just 1.1, which indicates relatively low amounts of leverage. Overall, TE has a fortress balance with strong FCF ability, which is the key appeal of the stock.

Figure 6: TE Connectivity's Key Cash Flow Generation Metrics, Source: TE Connectivity 10-K

Q2 FY2026 Expectations

For the upcoming Q2 FY2026 results, management has guided for $4.7 billion in sales (+13% YoY) and $2.65 in adjusted EPS (+20% YoY). Currently, consensus estimates are slightly more aggressive, as analysts are projecting $4.76 billion in revenue and EPS of $2.70. Historically, TE has a strong track record of beating consensus expectations, with EPS beats for the past four consecutive quarters. However, I would be more cautious about the probability of a raise for the 2H FY2026 due to the US-Iran war. With raw material and crude oil prices at elevated levels, this could pressure many of TE's customers in the industrial and automotive sectors. While the Industrial Solutions segment may remain resilient due to the AI-driven demand in its DDN segment, I expect management's tone to shift from the optimism seen in Q1 to a more guarded risk-monitoring stance. The critical question for the call will be whether the auto sector will see worsening pressure from already tepid demand going into the year, although CPV growth can help to mitigate some of this downside risk.

Figure 7: TE's Q1 Earnings Highlight and Q2 Financial Guidance, Source: TE's Q1 FY2026 Investor Presentation

Valuation

On the valuation front, TE's 22.2x forward FY2026 P/E ratio does not appear to be expensive. Compared to high-flying AI component stocks, which are trading at 30-40x forward P/E, this appears to be much more reasonable. However, I would argue that it is fair for TE to be trading at this level, given that AI only accounts for 10% of its business and that the majority of its revenue is still mainly from the Transportation segment. Although this could materially increase when TE's AI revenue reaches $3 billion in 2027, it would still account for under 20% of revenue if we benchmark it against consensus projections.

Figure 8: Consensus Estimates for TE Connectivity (FY2026-2028), Source: Quartr (Data by S&P Global)

If we compare TE with its peers, the most directly comparable company is Amphenol, considering that they both supply connectors in similar end markets (Transportation, Data Centers, Defense, etc.). Comparatively, TE seems to be trading at a discount to Amphenol, which is trading at 32.8x FY2026 P/E. However, since Amphenol has a relatively greater AI exposure (IT datacom accounts for 30+% of revenue), this valuation premium is justified. Meanwhile, Aptiv, which is mainly exposed to automotive connectors, is being punished by the market due to the tepid auto market growth. Overall, TE's relative valuation is reasonable; TE offers a superior product mix and higher AI exposure than Aptiv while remaining a cheaper alternative to the more expensive Amphenol.

Figure 9: Peer Analysis of TE Connectivity and Key Metrics, Source: Quartr (Data by S&P Global)

Risks to the Company

There are several risks that could affect my HOLD thesis. TE's main risk is the potential slowdown in Hyperscaler AI capex, which would materially affect its AI and DDN growth story. Next, the company has a risk of Technical obsolescence from the potential technological change from copper-based to optical-based networking solutions, which could render the company's backplane connector products obsolete. Finally, the company has relatively high exposure to the automotive sector due to its Transportation Solutions segment; if the downturn in the automotive sector continues to persist for a longer than expected duration, it could pose a significant downside to the stock.

Conclusion

In conclusion, TE has a structural growth story underpinned by the growing demand for power and data connectivity in AI servers, as well as the rising CPV in its Automotive revenue. While the company is seeing greater contributions from its AI story, with AI revenues expected to double to $3 billion in 2027, there is technical uncertainty from the upcoming CPO adoption, which may be an upcoming headwind for the stock. Although the company is preparing for this eventuality with its own CPO/CPC solutions, the lingering question is whether its solution will be adopted. Meanwhile, its Transportation Solutions segment is facing near-term headwinds from the industry cyclical downturn. Long-term, this segment is benefitting from the structural growth in CPV, which will drive mid-single-digit YoY growth; however, the growth outlook is relatively unattractive compared to its AI opportunity. At a 22x forward FY2026 P/E, the stock is fairly valued given its relatively low AI exposure and high automotive exposure. Unless there is an inflection point in global automotive production or greater certainty in its AI story, I am initiating the stock with a Hold call in the meantime.

data.title: Bitcoin's Price Outlook: Bitcoin Shrugs Off Sluggishness And Targets Recent Highs. Is $80000 A Possibility?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has displayed impressive resilience during the Monday session, shaking off early-morning sluggishness to reclaim the $76,000 handle. After a brief period of consolidation, the premier cryptocurrency looks poised to challenge its recent highs, underpinned by a technical structure that continues to favor the "buy the dip" crowd.

Daily Chart: Holding the MA High Ground

The daily timeframe remains the cornerstone of the current bullish thesis. Following the impulsive "V-shaped" recovery throughout early April, Bitcoin has successfully turned previous resistance into rock-solid support.

Key observations on the Daily:

  • The SMA Support Sandwich: Bitcoin is currently trading comfortably above its 100-day MA (yellow) at $74,145 and its 50-day MA (blue) at $70,577.

As long as the pair remains above this "support sandwich," the broader bias remains firmly bullish.

  • The $75,000 Pivot: The daily candles are showing a consistent ability to close above the $75,000 psychological level, suggesting that institutional interest is picking up at these elevated levels.
  • RSI Momentum: The daily RSI is trending at 61, indicating that while momentum is positive, we are still a long way from the "danger zone" of 70+, leaving significant room for a run toward the $82,133 hurdle.

Bitcoin (BTC/USD) Daily Chart, April 20, 2026

H4 Chart: The Bullish Base at $74000

Zooming into the H4 chart, we can see a textbook example of healthy trend development. After hitting a local top near $78,197, the pair underwent an orderly retracement that found a floor exactly at the 50-period MA (blue), currently at $74,632.

The H4 structure has now printed a significantly higher low. With the RSI bouncing off its midpoint (58) after a "PIVOT" low signal, the indicators suggest that the corrective phase is over, and the next impulsive leg may be beginning to take shape.

Bitcoin (BTC/USD) Four-Hour Chart, April 20, 2026

H1 Chart: Session Scenarios & Intraday Outlook

The hourly chart provides the most immediate optimism, with Bitcoin slicing back above its 50, 100, and 200-period MAs in a single concerted move.

The Bullish Scenario

For the bulls to maintain this momentum into the Asian and European sessions, we need to see a sustained hold above the $75,700 area (the H1 100-MA). A clean break above $76,800 would likely trigger a liquidation of short positions, clearing the path for a retest of $78,197. If buying pressure persists, a psychological run toward $80000 becomes the primary target.

The Bearish Scenario

The bears need a rejection at current levels and a break back below the $75,000 pivot to regain any short-term control. Failure to hold the $74,555 level (H1 200-MA) would signal a more prolonged consolidation, likely drawing the price back toward the structural support at $71673.

However, given the current "BULL" labels on the RSI, the bears seem to be on the back foot for now.

Key Levels to Watch:

  • Resistance: $78,197, $80,000, $82,133
  • Support: $75,000, $74,145 (Daily 100-MA), $71,673

Bitcoin (BTC/USD) One-Hour Chart, April 20, 2026

Bitcoin is effectively "re-loading" for its next major move. The confluence of support between $74,000 and $75,000 has proven to be a formidable base for the bulls. Will it serve as a base for Bitcoin to finally push beyond the coveted $80,000 mark?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Union Pacific: 2 Key Dates Are Coming (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Introduction

After a week where Financials set the tone of the earnings season, I am going back to Industrials. After all, these sectors have usually been highly correlated as industrial cycles are financed and create a tailwind for the banks. Until recently. Right now, we have seen a rare decoupling among Financials (XLF) and Industrials (XLI) that is now apparently ending. When we add the fact that oil is the hot topic right now, it becomes extremely interesting to look at what the main Class 1 railroads are going to report this week. Specifically, let's deal with Union Pacific (UNP), the largest publicly traded railroad and the acquiring carrier in the $85B pending bid for Norfolk Southern (NSC). When the deal was announced, I shared an article on a possible way to play the arbitrage opportunity, where the best option was to go long NSC and, eventually, go short UNP, in case the main goal was just to capture the spread. So, it was not a bear call on UNP, since I have been structurally bullish over the long-term prospects of North American Class 1 railroads.

Union Pacific's Last Financials

The first time I ever wrote about North American railroads was when I shared my framework article, where I shared what I learned from studying how Buffett invested in BNSF. I saw the metrics he used to assess the deal were pre-tax interest coverage (Buffett doesn't like EBITDA), return on incremental capital, fuel efficiency (used as a proxy of effective management), and shareholder returns.

As we get ready to read and assess Union Pacific's upcoming earnings report, let's take a moment to recap where Union Pacific is. Last year, its revenues were $24.5B. However, Class 1 railroads can have a volatile top-line not only because of volume cyclicality, but also because of fuel surcharges. When fuel prices go up, these railroads can charge an additional fee, usually with a 60-day lag. In FY25, freight revenue was $23.22, split into three categories: bulk (33% of freight revenue and 24% of carloads), industrial (37% of the freight revenue and 27% of the carloads), and premium (30% of the freight revenue and 49% of the carloads). We can immediately see that bulk is the most valuable category in terms of revenue per carload. The main economic drivers that impact Union Pacific's revenues are four: industrial production, coal demand, intermodal (container volumes at West Coast ports), and, as said, fuel surcharges.

Since the company saw Vincenzo Vena stepping in as the new CEO, Union Pacific has quickly climbed up the ranks of the industry and is now the best one in terms of operating ratio, being the only one that is now below 60%. This also goes at part with a rising ROIC, currently at 16.3%, which paints the picture of a company that is more efficient and more focused on earning a high rate of return on the capital it deploys.

As far as the UNP-NSC merger goes, in early January, the STB rejected the application because it was incomplete. A few weeks later, UNP wrote to the STB, informing it that it would have refiled by the end of April. This means that, during the earnings call, which will be held just seven days before the refiling has to be submitted, a few questions during the earnings call could be about this topic. Now, as far as I see it, the refiling actually increases the chances of the approval, especially if it will be accepted. On the other hand, if the filing is rejected a second time, the $2.5B fee would be activated.

With this in mind, let's look ahead and see what Union Pacific's report may look like.

Union Pacific's Q1 2026 Earnings Preview

A few months ago, during the Q4 2025 earnings call, Union Pacific's management disclosed an initial outlook for 2026. The macroeconomic outlook that Union Pacific had framed in January didn't plan for a big economic upswing, with industrial revenues seen under pressure as auto sales were expected to be weak, as well as housing starts. On the other hand, coal was expected to do well, as well as grain products. In this environment, Union Pacific said that investors should not expect pricing to be margin accretive in 2026, although it is always above inflation, on an absolute dollar basis. UNP expected its operating ratio to improve as a result of better efficiency. To this extent, we need to consider its $3.3B CapEx plan, which ranks, among its top priorities, infrastructure maintenance and locomotive modernization.

The result is that UNP is guided for mid-single-digit EPS growth, which means that FY26 EPS should be around $12.58. As far as the NSC merger goes, UNP told us that we need to be patient and wait until 1H27.

However, this guidance has now aged since the world has changed quite a bit in the past three months.

First of all, UNP guidance expected a price per gallon of $2.35. Clearly, this is way off now, as the estimated quarterly price I am considering is around $4.00/gallon. This is over 70% above the January guidance and requires that investors handle this change correctly. There will be a gross impact of $1.65 per gallon, which could be equal to $400M in additional fuel expense (consider roughly 235M gallons consumed during Q1). Surely, the fuel surcharge program will mitigate the impact, but the offset has a lag and, therefore, higher fuel prices in March will only be fully absorbed in April and May.

We also have the possibility to check the volume forecast. In fact, we can use the weekly and monthly data that the Association of American Railroads (AAR) releases. Below, we see that the YoY change in U.S. total rail carloads is actually favorable, as we see all three Q1 months in the positive.

We can also use the U.S. rail traffic release for the first 12 weeks of the year. We see that total traffic increased by 1.7%, with the only two negative categories being forest products and intermodal units. On the other hand, grain is particularly strong (+17.8%). Coal is also up 3.3%, but I think that the real effect of the Iran war on this commodity will be seen in April. This makes me think that Union Pacific could report higher volumes compared to the previous guidance.

Moreover, I think there will also be a positive surprise in the industrial category. In fact, although UNP expected softer activity, the ISM manufacturing PMI has moved into expansion territory for the first quarter since 2022. I would not be surprised to hear that UNP is updating its guidance, moving the needle up for certain commodities such as chemicals or metals. Intermodal, on the other hand, will be carefully monitored. I think that if it reports a decrease that doesn't go below 5%, everything is going to be fine. But if intermodal is down by over 5% to a number close to 10%, then UNP might have a traffic problem.

We said above that we also have to consider the Buffett criteria. Well, at the end of FY26, pre-tax interest coverage was 7x, which is more than acceptable. But the Q1 print could report a compressed ratio because of the incremental fuel costs that won't be fully offset yet. Fuel efficiency was at 1.072 gallons per thousand GTMs, a 1% reduction YoY and the best ever consumption rate reported by the company. This shows that Vena's team is managing the railroad well. While in Q1 we will see a fuel cost explosion, if UNP's fuel efficiency stays this low or even improves, we will have another strong signal of the company's internal efficiency.

Union Pacific's Valuation

I don't own UNP. I had NSC, but I exited it a few months ago because I needed to raise cash for higher-conviction/higher-returns picks (such as Netflix). The stock was above $260 before the outbreak of the Iran war. Now it is on its way to recover and reclaim the 52-week high. Its valuation multiples are reasonable: a fwd PE of 20.2 and an EV/EBITDA ratio of 13.7 show that Union Pacific is priced more or less in line with the general market. The dividend yield is still above 2%, and Vena has committed to higher shareholder returns. It is hard for me to expect a deep bear case. We would need Union Pacific to report an operating ratio above 63% or to cut its 2026 EPS guidance by 20%. For sure, on a pullback to $230, the stock would be trading at a fwd PE of 18.3, which would make the entry point quite interesting. This is why I am giving UNP a buy rating: over the long term, I am bullish on the company. However, over the next few days, I would stay on the sidelines to wait for a better entry point in order to enter with a higher margin of safety, especially when two big dates are approaching (the earnings call the refiling deadline) that could generate some volatility in the stock.

data.title: Zions Bancorporation: Solid Q1 Results
data.description:
We are early in our coverage of the Q1 regional bank earnings season. We believe reviewing the earnings of regional banks is a great way to do what we call a "pulse check" on the strength of the economy both locally and regionally. Thus far, we have seen overall stability across the sector in the number of reports we have reviewed. It has been a good sample thus far of large and smaller banks, and thus far we just aren’t seeing any evidence of a slowdown in borrowing or ability of borrowers to pay their loans. We thought we might see this because of the macro concerns in March with the war and gas prices surging. But so far, it is not reflected in the data we have checked so far.

In today's column, we turn our attention to Zions Bancorporation, National Association (ZION). This one is based out of Salt Lake City and operating across 11 Western states. So this bank gives us another sample of data to help better understand the economic health of the Mountain West and Pacific regions. After reviewing the just-reported Q1 results, we see a bank with good earnings growth and solid credit metrics. Let us check in on and discuss the key metrics we follow.

Zions Bancorporation Q1 Earnings Headline Results

In the just-reported Q1, Zions delivered a strong performance on the top and bottom lines, in our opinion. Total revenue hit $849 million, up 7.4% year-over-year. The provision for credit losses was notable as it was a $7 million recovery versus an actual $18 million a year ago. This helped boost earnings, and this is because the provisions directly impact earnings. Net income was $232 million, or $1.56 per share. Folks, this represents a massive 38% increase from the $1.13 reported a year ago. It's also up from $1.48 in the sequential quarter. It beat estimates by $0.13.

Zions Financial Q1 Loans And Deposits

We always look into the trends in loans and deposits when we do a bank earnings review. And for both of these key metrics, trends are moving in the right direction. Loans grew to $61.3 billion, a 2% increase from the prior year. This growth was driven by an $860 million increase in commercial loans and a $458 million increase in consumer loans, particularly in home equity lines. We also saw total deposits up to $76.9 billion. This was stable growth of 2% year-over-year. As you can see, the company has a long runway of deposits to lend against. Overall these trends were good in our opinion.

Zions Financial Q1 Asset Quality And Return Metrics

Asset quality at Zions is among the cleanest we have seen this season, even though it's early in our coverage of Q1 results. But it was really good. The annualized ratio of net loan charge-offs to average loans was a mere 0.03%, down from 0.11% a year ago. Further, the so-called classified loans that are at risk dropped by 19% over the past year to $2.3 billion. A lot of this was due to reductions in commercial real estate exposures. Also, nonperforming assets were down to $292 million. This was down to 0.48% of total loans, a decrease from the 0.51% reported last year.

Margins were up nicely, too. The net interest margin came in at 3.27%, up from 3.10% a year ago, though it did edge down slightly from the sequential Q4. The improvement was due to lower funding costs as rates have come down, while yields on loans have held up. If there is one gripe we have with the bank's performance, it is that it is not as efficient as many others. It is average at best. The good news is that the bank’s efficiency ratio improved to 65.0% from 66.6% last year, but the best banks we cover from this standpoint are under 50% on this metric.

Final Thoughts On Zions

Zions Bancorporation’s Q1 report adds to the evidence base thus far that despite all of the fear over the economy and the pain of gas and utility prices, there are no signs of a slowdown in borrowing or asset quality. We are seeing a return to growth in both loans and customer deposits, paired with some of the best credit metrics we have seen so far. While it is not as efficient as other banks, the company is a quality operator. There are repurchases here and, of course, a dividend, which is nice to have when owning any stock. We would like to be buyers a little closer to book value, which was up to $41.75, so at this level we rate it a Hold.
